title: Financial Aid Inquiry
transcript: Student: Hi, I'm calling because my financial aid package for next semester seems lower than expected. Could someone help me understand it? Officer: I'd be happy to help. Can you give me your student ID? Student: It's 556-7734. Officer: I see. Your aid was reduced because your expected family contribution increased based on the new tax documents you submitted. Student: But my family's income didn't go up. We just sold a property, which was a one-time thing. Officer: I understand. A change in assets can affect the calculation. You can file an appeal explaining that the property sale was a non-recurring event. Student: How do I do that? Officer: You'll need to submit a Financial Aid Appeal Form along with a letter from your family explaining the situation and any supporting documents. Student: How long does the appeal take? Officer: Typically two to three weeks. If approved, your aid will be adjusted before the tuition deadline. Student: That's a relief. I'll start the appeal today. Officer: Good. You can find the form on our website under Appeals and Special Circumstances.

questions.stem: Why does the student contact the financial aid office?
options: To apply for a new scholarship
To understand why their aid was reduced
To report a change in major
To request a tuition payment plan
questions.explanation: The student's financial aid package is lower than expected, and they want to understand why.

questions.stem: What does the officer suggest the student do?
questions.explanation: The officer suggests filing an appeal explaining that the property sale was a non-recurring event.

audioSegments.text: Hi, I'm calling because my financial aid package for next semester seems lower than expected. Could someone help me understand it?
I'd be happy to help. Can you give me your student ID?
I see. Your aid was reduced because your expected family contribution increased based on the new tax documents you submitted.
But my family's income didn't go up. We just sold a property, which was a one-time thing.
I understand. A change in assets can affect the calculation. You can file an appeal explaining that the property sale was a non-recurring event.
You'll need to submit a Financial Aid Appeal Form along with a letter from your family explaining the situation and any supporting documents.
How long does the appeal take?
Typically two to three weeks. If approved, your aid will be adjusted before the tuition deadline.
That's a relief. I'll start the appeal today.
Good. You can find the form on our website under Appeals and Special Circumstances.
